name: Scala 3 Macros
summary: Scala 3 redesigned metaprogramming around `inline def` (function bodies inlined at typecheck) and quoted macros (`'{e}` quotations + `${e}` splices, MetaOCaml-inspired). Replaces Scala 2's deprecated whitebox/blackbox macros with a disciplined two-tier system.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Scala 3 redesigned metaprogramming around two primitives:

- **`inline def`**: functions whose bodies are inlined at the call site during typechecking. Arguments can be marked `inline` to force compile-time evaluation. Unlike C++ `inline`, Scala's is semantic — certain operations only work on inline values (e.g., `constValue`).
- **Quoted macros**: expression-level quotations (`'{ e }`) and splices (`${ e }`) inspired by MetaOCaml. A macro is a quoted-code-producing function run at compile time. Typed throughout — `Expr[Int]` is distinct from `Int`.

The design goal was to replace Scala 2's ad-hoc whitebox and blackbox macros (deprecated for portability reasons) with a disciplined two-tier system.

sections.title: inline vs quoted
sections.content:
**inline def** is the simple case. The compiler substitutes the body at the call site at typecheck time, with `inline` parameters becoming compile-time-known values. Pattern-matching on `inline` values is permitted; `compiletime.constValue[T]` retrieves a literal-typed value at compile time. This handles 80% of Scala 2's macro use cases (specialised arithmetic, type-level computation, default values) without a full macro system.

**Quoted macros** handle the remaining 20%. A quoted macro is a function `def myMacro(x: Expr[Int])(using Quotes): Expr[Int]` that takes typed code and returns typed code. The `Quotes` context provides operations on the AST: pattern-matching on expressions, generating fresh symbols, calling the type checker. Output expressions are validated as part of macro execution, so generated code that doesn't type-check fails at the macro's call site rather than during a separate post-pass.

sections.title: Compared with Scala 2 macros
sections.content:
Scala 2's macro system had two flavours: **blackbox** (the macro's return type matches the declared signature) and **whitebox** (the macro's return type can differ from the signature, enabling runtime-shaped APIs). Whitebox macros were powerful but unportable across Scala versions and incompatible with much tooling.

Scala 3's quoted macros are blackbox by design — the return type is part of the signature. The expressivity loss versus whitebox is mostly recoverable through type-class derivation (`derives` clauses) and the inline/quote split. Early reports from libraries ported between Scala 2 and 3 suggest the new system is more predictable but somewhat more verbose.
